mnemonics.title: Combination Formula Song
mnemonics.content: n choose r equals n factorial, divided by r factorial times n minus r factorial - just remember C(n,r) = n!/(r!(n-r)!)

mnemonics.title: Pascal's Triangle Building
mnemonics.content: Row by row, start and end with 1; every number in between is the sum of the two above it - like a pyramid of numbers growing!

mnemonics.title: Binomial Theorem Key
mnemonics.content: (a+b) to the n equals sum of nCk times a to the n-k times b to k; the coefficients come from Pascal's triangle, you see!

acronyms.expansion: n Choose r / n Select r
acronyms.usage: Represents the number of ways to choose r objects from n distinct objects without considering order
Mathematical notation for combinations, also written as nCr or nCk
The (k+1)th term in binomial expansion is nCk × a^(n-k) × b^k

visualTips.visualization: Draw a triangular array with 1 at the top. Each number below is the sum of two numbers above it (left and right). Row 0: 1; Row 1: 1 1; Row 2: 1 2 1; Row 3: 1 3 3 1; Row 4: 1 4 6 4 1. These are the binomial coefficients!

For (a+b)^n, write coefficients from Pascal's row n, then decrease power of a from n to 0 while increasing power of b from 0 to n. Example for n=3: coefficients 1,3,3,1 give a^3 + 3a^2b + 3ab^2 + b^3.
nCr and nC(n-r) are equal - picture it as choosing r to include is same as choosing (n-r) to exclude from n items.

analogies.analogy: Selecting a committee (combinations) is like choosing players for a team where order doesn't matter, versus lining up students (permutations) where who stands first, second, third matters.
Think of Pascal's Triangle like stacking blocks - each block rests on two blocks below it, and its value is the sum of those two supporting blocks.
The binomial theorem is like a recipe: ingredients are a and b, the power n tells you how many total ingredients to use, and the combination formula tells you how many ways to mix them!
